prediction.direction: Train a small audio language model using a vector-quantized bottleneck with a codebook size equal to the phoneme inventory of the target language, using only a few hours of transcribed speech and no external teacher or large pretraining data.
prediction.rationale: The phoneme count of any language is small (≈40), making the audio-to-text mapping intrinsically low-dimensional. A quantized bottleneck forces the model to retain only linguistically relevant features, so a small dataset suffices when the codebook is properly initialized. Recent advances in contrastive objectives stabilize VQ training with small data, enabling this under-explored regime.
prediction.builds_on: Enough of Scaling LLMs! Lets Focus on Downscaling
prediction.persistent_gap: No existing method trains a small audio language model to competitive accuracy without large pretraining data or a large teacher model.
prediction.why_still_open: Prior small-model methods (e.g., HuBERT, wav2vec 2.0) rely on large unlabeled datasets for self-supervised pretraining, or on distilling a large teacher. They have not demonstrated that small models can be trained from scratch with only a small labeled dataset.
prediction.bridge_keep: The bottleneck quantization mechanism that filters redundant acoustic variability.
prediction.bridge_remove: Reliance on large data or a large teacher to learn the quantizer; replace with a phoneme-constrained codebook and a contrastive loss that works with small data.
prediction.bridge_add: Train a small VQ-VAE where the codebook size is set to the phoneme count of the target language, initialized uniformly. Use a contrastive loss (InfoNCE) between audio segments and their quantized representations, combined with a small supervised cross-entropy loss on transcriptions. The encoder is a small convolutional network. This removes the need for a large external resource by leveraging the structural prior that linguistic content is low-dimensional and discrete.
prediction.non_obvious_property: Property: The codebook size equals the phoneme inventory (≈40), which is a small, known constant. This forces the model to learn exactly the set of linguistic units, eliminating any need for large data to learn redundant variations. Why prior work cannot exploit it: Prior VQ-based models (e.g., VQ-VAE, HuBERT) use large codebooks (128+ entries) learned from large data; they treat codebook size as a hyperparameter, not as a fixed linguistic prior. Why composition fails: A naive combination of VQ-VAE with a small codebook on a small dataset suffers from codebook collapse (all inputs map to one token) because the typical VQ-VAE training relies on large batch sizes and data diversity. Our method adds a contrastive loss that forces each codebook vector to be distinct and well-separated, a non-trivial addition that is not present in standard VQ-VAE or any prior VQ+contrastive combination.
prediction.obvious_composition_check: Shortest description: 'VQ-VAE with phoneme-informed codebook for small-data audio language modeling'. This description is incomplete because it omits the contrastive loss stabilization that is essential to prevent collapse; without it, a standard VQ-VAE with a small codebook on small data fails. The load-bearing piece is the contrastive loss, which is not part of the naive composition. Therefore, the composition is materially wrong.
prediction.forest_meta_gap: Both trees fail to establish that small models can achieve target performance without access to large pretraining data or a large teacher model.
prediction.forest_shared_mechanism: Reliance on a large external resource that provides knowledge or data; no method has been validated to work entirely without that resource.
